verbatim_text: I'm excited to share my work on building collaborative language model agents, and in particular, for a more cohesive through line, we're going to be talking about AI assisted software development in this talk, but hopefully there's more takeaways that will be applicable to other use cases or applications that folks might be interested in. Okay, so to make sure we're all on the same page in terms of what AI technology looks like in software development, let's look at this short demo. So I asked the agent, build me a tool to keep track of the faculty. I'll meet at Cornell CS and you can see that the agent is reading project structure. For a caveat, I cut up this video so it's sped up and also sliced together. You know your agents don't work this quickly, but you can see that the agent has created a bunch of files, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and it has started a server to check what the files look like or running this little application and seeing what that actually looks like. And this is the little app that it builds. And as you can see, I verified I think all of these people do exist in some capacity on the Cornell website. So no hallucinated faculty members, but I was surprised that the agent was telling me I should be meeting with the provost of computer science rather than asking me maybe what are my research interests and trying to match faculty with that. So maybe a little room to go in terms of working together with the agent to actually build out the right set of meetings and so on. But as you can see from this small example where models aren't working in this like standalone fashion, you know we see new announcements from frontier model providers of new models like G5, Gemini 3, and in particular the one powering that little demo was Cloud Opus 4.6. Rather they're actually participating in these collaborative workflow through tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Cloud Code, and other kinds of applications. And from this we also are seeing increasingly examples where the performance that is reported in one setting may not match the experiences of users in other settings. So when Gemini 3 came out late last year it touted state-of-the-art performance compared to many other pure models at that time from Google's competitors out performing them on a well-known benchmark called cbench and which tests models abilities to fix bugs and resolving issues and code bases.
